event: Adam and Eve Discover the Body of Abel
symbols: blood-red sun — the cosmic horror of the first human death, creation itself groaning under the curse of sin
naked bodies — the primal state of humanity stripped of innocence, now confronting death for the first time
Abel's lifeless body — the first martyr, innocent blood crying from the ground, prefiguring Christ's atoning blood
Adam's anguished posture — the father of humanity bearing the full weight of the Fall's consequences
Eve's horror — the mother of all living encountering death, the bitter fruit of the serpent's deception
dark mountains — the hostile, cursed earth that now receives innocent blood
description: Two nude figures — a powerfully muscled man and a stout woman — react with visceral anguish upon finding the pale, lifeless body of a young man stretched on the ground. Adam strides forward with arms flung wide, his face contorted in horror and grief. Eve sits beside the corpse, her mouth open in a scream, hands raised in despair. A large blood-red disc (the sun) glows ominously behind a dark mountainous landscape. The palette is dominated by deep greens, ochres, and fiery reds, rendered in Blake's characteristic visionary manner.
theological_description: Blake's painting captures the moment when Adam and Eve — the first parents — discover the slain body of their son Abel, murdered by his brother Cain (Genesis 4:8). This is the first death in human history, and its horror is cosmic: death entered through Adam's sin (Romans 5:12), and now Adam himself must witness its firstfruits in his own child. The blood of Abel cries out from the ground to God (Genesis 4:10), a cry that Hebrews 12:24 contrasts with the blood of Jesus which 'speaks a better word.' Abel thus becomes the first in the line of righteous martyrs (Matthew 23:35), and his innocent death is a type of Christ's — the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world (Revelation 13:8). Eve's grief fulfills the curse of Genesis 3:16, where sorrow would multiply for the woman. The blood-red sun evokes the prophetic imagery of cosmic mourning — the sun darkened at Christ's crucifixion (Luke 23:44-45) — suggesting that all creation participates in the tragedy of sin's wages.

scripture_refs.reason: Cain rose up against Abel his brother and killed him — the murder whose aftermath is depicted.
The voice of Abel's blood crying to God from the ground — the theological center of the scene.
Through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin — Adam now sees death's reality.
The blood of Jesus speaks a better word than the blood of Abel — Abel's blood as type of Christ's.
From the blood of righteous Abel — Jesus names Abel as the first in the line of martyrs.
The Lamb slain from the foundation of the world — Abel's death as a prefiguration of Christ's sacrifice.
The curses of the Fall now realized in grief, toil, and death within Adam and Eve's own family.
The sun darkened at Christ's death — the blood-red sun echoes cosmic mourning over innocent blood.

_meta.title: The Body of Abel Found by Adam & Eve
